speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Louis Boas took place at his home in Moscow on July 30th, 1976. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Probably the best. He went back to Professor Baker's drama school at the University of Moscow. Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Talbot?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You've heard of him, have you?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yes.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: And of course later he went to, he wrote several plays and they were produced in England mostly. And then he joined the crew at Hollywood and became very familiar with, I can't think of the name, Norma Shearer and her husband. And he wrote about three or four movies that won Oscars, All Quiet. No.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Nute me on the Bounty. Nute me on the Bounty, yes. Nute me on the Bounty with the Charles Lawton and then Across the White Missouri and Romeo and Juliet. He adapted for the movies and The Good Earth and The Black Pearl, which did not succeed as well as the rest. But he had three or four. Northwest Passage, he wrote that and about four other Boaz masters. He's still living at West Glacier, Montana.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We get a Christmas card from him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who else was in that group that went up to write for, or made a living in English?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: There was Blaine Stubblefield, who became editor of a motor and travel magazine, I can't tell you the name of it, in New York. And then he got tired of the rat race and came out to Weiser, Idaho, and freelance. And he was a great promoter of the Hells Canyon as a recreation area. I believe he's died now a few years ago. And Joel L. Priest, who went to work for the Salt Lake Tribune and then became the public relations man and writer for the Union Pacific Railroad. He's retired in Carmel, California.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: And my wife, who did a little freelancing, and I entered the newspaper business after being a boost writer for Westinghouse Electric at Pittsburgh. I spent a year with them and then I couldn't stand Pittsburgh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't there one fellow who went on to Stanford?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Yes.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Buck, the U .C .K. I can't think of his first name now. Philip Buck, who went on to Stanford and wrote some, oh he was a, wrote work of a higher class than we did, you know. A Ph .D. type writing papers and brochures on obscure subjects that English teachers write about, if you understand what I mean.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Analyzing bail for some particular abstract thought that was wooden C in it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the English department at the university at that time was strong?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Very strong, very strong.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: George Boring Miller was the head of the department and he was a martinet, but he had a good department. And of course John Cushman, who's still living, he's 85, and he lives in Moscow for nine months of the year. He was the one who attracted the people that were interested in writing. He had what he called a, a creative writing course, and he attracted those people to his course. And he encouraged Talbot Jennings to move on with his writing. He encouraged me to move on. And another at that era was Orrin Fitzgerald, who worked for the Salt Lake Tribune, and later came to Moscow as agricultural editor. And he wrote literally hundreds of articles for agricultural publications. He worked for a short time after he was here at the university. He left and went back to Boston as the editor of a publication for the Sheet Growers Association or something. And he didn't like Boston. He was purely a western man and gave it up and came back to Idaho. But he was a staff writer for several agricultural publications.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: One was, is it the American Farmer put out by the folks of the Saturday Evening Post?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: I can't remember the name of it. It's still being published, but he was a staff writer for them but located in Moscow, and he had an article about once a month. And he wrote for agricultural publications in Denver. One of them was a cattle association magazine that Denver did. In the Idaho farmer, he wrote for the Idaho farmer, published in Spokane.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he did a lot of writing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say that George Orrin Miller was somewhat of a martinet, what do you mean by that? What kind of a dig?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Well, he ran the department with a marin hand. And if you made a mistake in English, he just climbed all over you. In other words, he wouldn't allow you to split an infinitive. And he wouldn't allow you to say the team they. And if you read the modern newspapers, that's 100 % what they do. The city council they did so and so. And my wife says that in the later years that became acceptable. Well, it never did to me. And when I was editor of the Idaho Union, I saw the story that the team they did so and so I corrected it and told the reporter that that was very potent. And my wife said, oh, they accept that now. I suppose the English language grows by usage. In other words, a team would be singular rather than quitter. Yes, it is singular.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In other words, what would you say with two teams?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, that's where.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Okay, what do you say with one team when you say they? Getting a point?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: I think it should be singular at all times. And I noticed in the newspapers they talk of the.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: So and so that was elected city councilman. Well, of course, it should be who. And then they will say that the horses who ran over the grade so far, there should be what or that they confuse the personal pronoun either leaving it out and using another reference word or use a personal pronoun when it should be the impersonal pronoun. It's done all the time. I see it in the lowest in tribute. I see it in all publications, except I don't see it in the Wall Street Journal, which is one publication that is factually correct.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I never noticed that in the journal.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, speaking about Miller, did he strike you as as he seemed to strike a lot of people in law school as being a character?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was a character. He was eccentric.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: No question. He was an eccentric character, although his majors adored him. He was a fine man and he insisted upon correct usage of the English language. No question about it. And they used to call him an eccentric because you had to buy a tablet of paper to use for your themes, which carried on the cover his rules for the usage of English. And he made a royalty on selling those. The freshman had to buy that booklet. And he was criticized for that, which is wrong. No question about it. I don't think a professor should have a racket of that nature where he makes you buy a certain publication because he's got a royalty on it. But he required an instilled in the freshman. At that time, we had two general lectures a week from George Maury Miller on English.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: All freshmen?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: All of them in one group. See the two were one. We all met in the auditorium. And he lectured the entire freshman class in English.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: On usage primarily?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Oh, usage and literature, whatever happened to be his topic. But we had a general meeting of all freshman English students. Was it a good... Yes, it was good. The way they do and I think they still do in chemistry. One, have a general lecture by a department man once a week. And then you go to your individual classes for additional lectures and questions and answers and quizzes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Let me get this straight. This was for the whole English... Freshman. Freshman. Freshman. All class.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How's that?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No matter what your major was. No, no. No matter what your major was.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Of course, freshman English was required. But the entire freshman class met once or twice a week. I can't remember which. In the auditorium for George Maury Miller's lecture on English. And then you spent two periods a week with your individual instructor in which you wrote things, answered oral quizzes and took your written quizzes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think at that time that the presence of men like Miller and Cushman had an effect on attracting students to English as a major?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: John Cushman did. He attracted people who were interested in writing, definitely. I don't know that George Maury Miller did, no. But we had a man here who was an outstanding authority on history.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Hume.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: H -U -M -E. He went from here to Stanford. And he was one of the, at least, United States authorities. And I think from the world authority on European history. And he was here in the early 20s and left about 1921. He was a fine man. And he attracted students here for history. And he became, he was recognized while he was here. And after he left, he was recognized as the worldwide authority on history. The University of Idaho now has two worldwide authorities in science. Dr. Peck in physics is a, he's a world authority on certain phases of physics. I had a friend of mine whose son graduated in physics in Stanford University and came here for his M .A. because he couldn't afford to go on Stanford anymore. And he was amazed to find that Dr. Peck was here as a member of the physics department when he had studied his book at San, at, at Palo Alto, at Stanford. My other, of course, worldwide authority is Miss Janine.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: She married Grever. Oh, she's a worldwide authority today on fluorocarbon chemistry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You remember who?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, I don't know her. You don't know her. No. Janine Shreve.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Janine Shreve. And she's traveled all over the world getting lectures on fluorocarbons. It's an accident that we got her. She came from Montana as an unknown. But as a young graduate, young Ph .D., and she was interested in fluorocarbons and she had her candidates for master's degrees and Ph .D.'s work on fluorocarbons and she became a world authority. I think we have a fewer percentage of those now than we had in 1920 because we had some very good teachers. We had A .W. Ferrenwald in mining who was still living. And he's a world authority on mining problems. He developed a flotation process for separating minerals from ores. And he's been called all over the world to consult with mining companies. He went to Cyprus a number of years ago and he's been retired for 20 years. And we had a better faculty overall than we have today.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think the difference has been in time?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't know. I don't know. I do not know.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Except that it was a small school. We had a small community. People liked the climate. I think we were paying a comparable wage at that time, which was nothing but what it is now. They got seven or eight thousand dollars a year, but now they get twenty five thousand dollars a year in Yale.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Except in English, I think they're in demand more than they were then. We've had an explosion in a number of students all over the world. It's gotten to the point that if you don't go to college, you don't amount to anything that's wrong.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wondered if the competition among the three Idaho schools would have an effect as well because of the demands from BSU and ISU for funds. I'm sure the demand that I'm sure the three institutions do mean that we have to spread it thinner.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: We have to spread our support for higher education for three institutions rather than one. That's why it was. It was one then. But you know, as Idaho State College was known then as Idaho Technical Institute, it was purely a technical institute where you learned barbering and cosmetology and auto mechanics and so forth. Then it became a branch of university, the southern branch. Then it became an independent institution, a two year institution, and a four year institution. Now it's giving graduate work and they want to give PhDs. If you had for 750 ,000 people, you can't afford that. It's like putting three degree granting institutions supported by the public money of the city of Seattle.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: It can't be done in my estimation.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that there was concern at the University of Idaho back in the 20s about this development?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Certainly.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: It's always been here as long as I've been around Moscow. We thought we had politically, Moscow people thought they'd solved the problem when they made Pocatello, the institution of Pocatello, was asking for college status instead of an institute. It was accepted by the legislature as a branch of the university. It became a two year college. So we thought we'd solved that. They were under us. We controlled it. We sent a dean from Moscow to Pocatello to operate it. The Angel, whose daughter lives as two doors over here, Mr. Dusseau. We thought we'd solved it because it was part of us, a southern branch. We could hold it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: They got the bigger population and they got the people down there. They voted to make it an independent institution, to make it a four year school. Of course Boise started out as a junior college with a junior college district, which encompassed all of Ada County as a tax in units, you see. I think it took in some smaller areas around Ada County, probably part of the Payette County and so forth, as a tax in units. They supported that as a junior college. Well, you know, you get 75 ,000 people in an area with another 75 ,000 in the environs in Athens, all well and Payette and so forth, but they wanted their own school, so they made it a four year school. And mark my words, I won't live to see it. You will. And Boise is the major institution in the state of Idaho.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No question about it.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Just ten days ago, the State Board of Education approved a course, a four year course, in legal, oh technology is something, to graduate people with enough legal knowledge that they could do the paperwork of the lawyers and let the lawyers free.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you read about that?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: I know, but I know the course they're talking about because I know... Just in the paper ten days ago, that's the beginning of the law school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You wait four years from now, and they'll say we've got this, why not have the law school follow it? How much do you think that the university here is protected by its alumni who have gone on to become some of the leaders?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Very little.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who are the leaders for advancing Boise College to graduate status and PhD status in the law school? Who are they?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: The lawyers of Boise who all graduated from the University of Idaho. They tried to move the law school down to Boise two years ago. They tried four years ago to move it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who are the prime movers?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: The lawyers of Boise. 90 % of who graduated right up here. So you can't depend upon that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In my estimation.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What does protect the university?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Oh, the fact that it's been here. It's the oldest school. It has prestige. It has age.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It won't protect it forever.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Boise's got the population of Boise itself. 100 ,000 people. Okay, and this is 180 ,000. Well, Boise's getting up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you feel ultimately it gets down to the question of how or just to be interested in cities?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Power of politics.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Power of politics. Boise's population gives them as many representatives in the Senate and the House as all of North Idaho.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: They're going to get what they want. It's bound to be.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It's bound to be.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: It's why Frank Church didn't get to be vice president. He comes from a little state.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No influence.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: That's why Swiker was chosen by Reagan because he could influence 110 delegates from Pennsylvania. Or so he thought.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It's power of politics. You know, it makes me think of the old story about the origination of the university here rather than down there. I'm just curious about the version of that that you've heard.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you hear of it as a way of keeping North Idaho within the state?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yes, definitely.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Idaho was on the verge of seceding and joining with Western Montana and Eastern Washington to form the state of Lincoln. In fact, there was a bill passed by the legislature to create that state, and it was vetoed by President Cleveland. Cleveland was president of Cleveland. It was vetoed. It passed. And there was no question about it. They demanded something up here in Moscow was one of the leading communities of North Idaho, Moscow and Lewiston. And they put it in Moscow. No question. We had a couple of smart men down there as representatives and so forth. One was Fourny, Judge Fourny. He was there and he was a smart man, and he persuaded them to give the University of Idaho. And they thought, well, that's minor importance. That'll mean 50 or 75 young people going to the university and so forth and so on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No question about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that's what they thought? That they didn't realize the implications?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, they did not.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Because state universities were not too popular. There was one in California and I suppose one in other states. Washington had one. It was a state before we were, but it wasn't considered any way big form.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You yourself were raised in Boise, weren't you?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How do you feel then about that? Do you feel divided loyalties at all?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No. No.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Because I hadn't been back to Boise since I was about 20 years old. Oh, I've been back for visits, but I came out from Ohio in 1910. And when I was 18, I came to the University of Idaho and stayed here for a couple of years. And the recession came along with 1920 and 1921. I knew my dad was a little hard pressed, so I went to work and earned my way through school from then on. And only back there one year I went to Westinghouse at Pittsburgh as a ghostwriter. I graduated as an engineer. And I knew when I graduated that I was not an engineer, but I had the degree. In other words, I didn't like engineering. I liked chemistry. I always have. But I didn't like the engineering and went with it, mathematics and the headache. So the fact that Westinghouse needed the writer, and I could understand the language without a job. And I wrote articles for technical and semi -technical publications, which carried the name of the scientist or engineer that did the work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wrote it.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: He did the work. He couldn't write. I couldn't do the work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You get my point?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: And the term had not been devised at that time. I'd never heard the term ghostwriter until much later. That's what I was. I was a technical writer. And I stayed in Pittsburgh for one year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did you leave?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Well, if you'd seen Pittsburgh in those days, you'd have known why. They've cleaned it up since then. But it was the dirtiest place in the world. And the plant where I worked was right out in the East End, Turtle Creek, or what they called East Pittsburgh, and populated with ethnic minorities, which commonly referred to as hunkeys or negroes. And we lived in another area six miles away, Wilkinsburg, which was a nice little town, but it was a dirty town. And I didn't have any friends there, and I had a girl out here, and that was that. So I quit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you feel that West was a place to be rather than the East?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Then I did, yes. When I graduated from college, I thought you had to go East to be anybody. If you wanted to amount to something, you had to get back East. So I went back East, and when I got there, I was disillusioned.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that was a common attitude among the college grads?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: I think it was. It was a place where the big money was. It was a place where life was interesting. It was a place where people advanced. There wasn't anything out here. East and Idaho, we didn't have any industries in the West. Now California is full of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But we didn't have those. But the attraction would be East rather than Seattle, Portland, California. Oh, yes.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: People came to California to retire, but there was no industry of any major import. I wanted to go East, being the big time, and so forth and so on. And one year there, and the dirt and the filth, it was just awful dirty. They had not cleaned it up. Air pollution was terrible. You couldn't sit on the grass, ever. You had to put a newspaper down to sit on the grass and just surf. And it was just awful. And I took it for one year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And it sounds as though the cities were socially disorganized too. Well, I didn't know.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: I was young, and I didn't know anybody. I had a roommate who was from up at Weite, one of the space boys, Ralph Space. He since died. There were three or four of them. They were all brilliant. And he was my roommate, and he left before I did. He stuck it out about eight or nine months, and then he had to get out here in the wild area of the Weite region and became a forester. And he was an electrical engineer, and I stayed a little longer and finally just gave it up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, let me go back to the decision of going to college and talk a little bit about the college years here. I'm curious about the way that it was known that you knew that you would be going to college.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is it a foregone conclusion?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, it wasn't. No.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: A minority of students at that time went to college. I don't know what the percentage was. 15 percent, 20. It was a minority. But I wanted to go to college, and my dad wanted to go to college. I was interested in chemistry. It was my favorite topic in my school. So I came up here to go be a chemist, and they told me the best route was chemical engineering. So we took chemical engineering.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had your, had either of your parents gone to college?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: No, neither of them. My dad was a candy maker. He had a store in Boise.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But there was a value that he placed on education. Oh, yes. There was a value that he placed on education. That's right.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: And my friends came to college, almost all of them. Those that I ran around with, almost all came to college.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The Moscow?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The girls.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: My group came to college. Ted Sherman, who lives in Moscow, is one of them. That's about the size of ones that are here. But we came to college. And we all finished.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there that express that I've heard about? Was that running at that time? The train? Train? The special?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, yes. Oh, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that like?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Well, of course, the North and South Highway had not been built until several years later. And the only way to get here was the Union Pacific Railway. And students did not have cars. I remember the first student that had a car on campus was from Twin Falls. It was about his junior year, and he was an oddity. Everybody looked up to him. His father was a judge, and he had a car.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What year was this mid?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: About 1922. He had gone to University of Illinois or University of Michigan, I forget which, for one or two years. I don't know if he flunked out or I don't know if he ran out of money or what, but he came to Idaho. And he was a hotshot, and he had an automobile, the first one in town. The first student who had an automobile. So when he came to the University, the dates were set, and they formed a special train. Starting in at possibly Idaho Falls, but at least Pocatello. And I'm thinking Idaho Falls, and it just came around the loop into Oregon, into Moscow. And it was a big train. They'd have ten or twelve Pullman cars and a diner and a baggage car and so forth and so on. And it was strictly segregated. Girls in one car and men in another. And the dean of women always rode the train. Dean French. She rode the train. She was chaperoned. At a certain time at night, if you were sitting in a seat talking to a girl in the girls' section, it was time to go to the men's car. And you got the hell out of there. And then of course at Christmas time, they formed another one and went south. And they came back. And in the spring, they had another one taking the student's own. That was that. Oh, that ran for years. Student special.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Dean French live in Boise or?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: No, she lived in Moscow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it was just to accompany the train?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Oh, yes, just to accompany the train. She was always on the train. She was a fine woman. Her date, she was the first superintendent of public instruction in Idaho. She was born at Haley. And years ago, she became county superintendent and finally state superintendent. And from that, she became dean of women at Idaho. She died about twelve, fifteen years ago. Way up in her eighties. She lived in Seattle later. And she built, she was a leader. She knew that students had to have recreational facilities. All we had in town was a picture show.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: And all college dances, almost every weekend, there was an all college dance. Usually in the old gymnasium, which is now the women's gym. And they had an all college dance. And each fraternity and sorority, and there were only half as many as there are now, had one dance a year, sometimes two. But she saw the need for another recreation area. And she built with her own money. The Blue Bucket Inn. She financed it, paid for it, hired a man in here, and ran it. And one of the things she had there was beer.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: And it was near beer to begin with then. Of course we still had prohibition. But she ran it. And later when prohibition was over, she had real beer there. But it was a student, a student placed together, two floors. Lower floor was a, oh soda fountain, chairs and tables where you could go and get a Coke or sandwich or cookies and things. And upstairs was a dance hall. It's located about now where the bookstore is. And after the university took it over, that same building was the bookstore for many years until they remodeled the student union and tore it down and built a modern bookstore there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she run this, really a service for the students rather than to make any sort of profit?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She went broke.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: She went broke. Well she tried to make money. She wanted to break even on it and she never did. And finally she went broke in it. She was losing money. And the university bailed her out. Don't tell the legislature sat in the south of Idaho, but they bailed her out and they bought it for not as much as she paid for. They bailed her out and she got most of her money back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then the university ran it for a few years as they blew up it. Well if it was a, really a socially important function for the university to serve, then they should bail it. That's right. That's right.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: It was possible. It was right. And they ran it for some years as a student, as a student hang out. And then the nest came into being and the perch came into being and there was no longer any need for it. And so they converted it into a student bookstore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it another major meeting place for the students at that time?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yes. Yes.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: It was a hang out, just the way the student union is now. You go down there and get a cup of coffee. Coke was more, root beer were more popular than coffee. Coffee is a later thing. But you drop in there and get a five -cent glass of Coca -Cola and hang around by a bag of peanuts and chat. And upstairs was a dance floor and they converted that and had many of the all -student dances up there on Saturday night. The fraternities and sororities would rent it for their big occasions, their pledge dance and sweetheart dance and so forth. It was considerably nicer than the gym. Yeah. Oh yes. Yes, yes. It was a nice place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Very nice.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Compromable to the present student union is not as big, but it was a good dance hall.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you tell me the story that you were telling me before about your effort to make sure that her picture stayed in the student union rather than the... Well, yes.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: We went up, my wife and I, went up to some function at the student union. And the main stairway going up to a landing and then coming back this way to go up to the main big hall of the student union at the landing spot hanging on the wall there was Dean French's picture, an oil painting. And we went up there one time and saw it was gone and I was curious.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't see it.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Oh, not many weeks later I was approached to make a donation to the performing arts building. And so I pledged $1000 to the performing arts along with a lot of other people in Moscow. And I happened to see Jim Lyle who was the alumni secretary on the street down at 3rd and Main and I just stopped him and I said, Jim, I noticed a week or two ago that the portrait of Dean French was no longer hanging in the student union building. He says, oh, he said, I hadn't noticed it. I said, well, it's not there. I don't know where it is. Maybe it's been moved. I says, I'm telling you now that I'm cutting my pledge in two for the performing arts building unless I get a satisfactory explanation of why they moved the picture. A number of years before that, Dean French was still alive. She was dead by this time. It was a movement started by the alumni through the alumni president and I don't know who he was to get an oil painting of Dean French to hang in the student union. The painting cost roughly $1500. It might have been $2500, I don't know, but at least $1500. It was done by an artist in Seattle where Dean French was then living. It was just a quiet movement to raise the money and we raised the money. I maybe gave $10 and a lot of others did. She came over to see it and we hung it in the student union. A couple of weeks went by after I saw Jim Lamm and I got a telephone call from President Hartung.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, I understand you're a little disturbed about something, Louie, would you come up and talk to me?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: I said, be glad to. So I went up and he said that Jim Lyle had told him what I had said. I said, I feel that way very strongly. He said, well, we have decided to put the photographs of all the deans in one room at the library and line the walls with the pictures of all the deans past and present. And so that will go there. And I said, yes, and those photographs usually are 8 by 10s. It cost about $7. And I said, this amount of Dean French was inexpensive at that time. Oil painting. And I said, it's fine to have the deans photographs in the library, but Dean French started with her own money, the blue bucket in, and went broke at it. She did it as a service to the students. That's the forerunner of the student union. Her photograph belongs in the student union. And I said, I believe in tradition. I said, there are buildings on the University of Columbia campus that are 200 years old and they're still there. They still have the same names. So and so is Hall. It's true in the University of London. It's true at Oxford. I've seen them. Hundreds of years old and they have the same name. I says, I believe in those traditions and there's you can have all the other deans in this one room. Dean Francis belongs in the place that she started as a student service. He says, I see your point. Well, a couple of weeks went by and Jim Lyle called me up and said, you're a picture of Dean Francis back in the student union. It's still there. It's not hanging in the same place. It's hanging right next to the for a theater.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She sounds like an extremely dedicated person. She was. She was.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: She had various rules. In other words, no girl could appear on campus in anything but a skirt. Except when the temperature got down below a certain degree in winter, ten above zero or zero or something, then they can wear slacks. Otherwise, they wore skirts. No question about it. You were home by nine o 'clock, weeknights. You're in your dormitory, your sorority period. Saturday night and Friday night, she were in by eleven thirty and Sunday night by seven. And it was a rule. No one questioned it. She was very strict. But the students as such disliked her. And as graduates, they loved her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's correct.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: I thought she was sweet. I didn't like her. And yet when she and she was always invited to every fraternity and every sorority dance not to invite Dean French was unthinkable.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: As while undergraduates?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: While undergraduates. Oh, yes. It was unthinkable. And some student would be assigned to escort her, and they did. Everybody in the house, every man in the house danced with her. Always you danced with her. Maybe you had to cut in, but you danced with her. And she appreciated it. It was a courtesy. She did this. She would announce to a sorority and a fraternity that I would like to be invited to dinner next Sunday. I want to give a little ten minute talk after dinner and so forth. So you invited her. And her talk at the dinner table was on table manners and common courtesy. You didn't see students after that shoveling food in their mouth with the wrong hand of holding their fork this way. She did that every fall. And she taught them manners. And it was part of the college career to learn to be ladies and gentlemen. No one does that now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where do you think the source of the dislike of her as undergraduates would come to student?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, she was too strict. She was too strict. She didn't want to be regulated in there.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Well, it's... They wouldn't stand for it now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Exactly. That's what I was thinking. Yes.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: But then her word was law. She had the authority. I suppose she had the authority given to her by the president that she was to supervise the morals of the campus and be dean of women and see that the women were in their residence hall at a certain time of night. And that the boys were gone at a certain time of night and that was that. And she ruled with an iron thumb. The girls loved her. Many of the boys, including myself...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The girls did like her undergraduates. Oh, yes. I was noticing in one of these blue buckets that some of the attitudes didn't seem very different from some of the attitudes today. I don't know whether the guy who was writing it was just being really daring.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But... See if I can find it. There's some stuff in here which struck me as being a bit racy for the time. This one little story of the local laundry man having scoured the inner chambers of some of the female boarding houses secured valuable information including the real meaning of the Grecian symbols.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Gamma Phi Beta, good petting bunch. That's the first one. Kappa Alpha Theta, keen after twilight. Chai Beta, come on boys. It just seemed to me from reading some of that that there was... I don't know if this is just on the boys part or not but there really seems to be the same kind of interest in the opposite sex as there is today. Sure.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: I think it was common knowledge that the girls of one sorority were a little more lenient than the girls of another sorority. Just the way they do today. Some sororities pick students, some pick good looking girls.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, that's true.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: The capitalists have always gotten students. They've been number one or number two or number three in student achievements all through the years until a few years ago they... It's not more than five or six that they have stopped releasing the grade averages of the sororities and fraternities. Before then they released the averages. The average of a sorority would be a 2 .49 and it became a habit that the kappas were always number one or number two. And so because of that and that alone the complaints that were made affected rushing the following fall they dropped it. But it's been true the kappas have always somehow gotten good students. They've also gotten very attractive girls. That's probably correct. That was this boy's opinion of the girls, the ones that would allow you to kiss them at night and so forth and so on and those that wouldn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's true.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: But Dean French was quite well, reasonably well liked by the girls and despised by the majority of the men. But ten years out of college you flubbed her. You knew she was right. You knew she gave you something. She gave you table manners.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: When a woman came into the room you stood up and she told... She gave us all these things, you know. And that's part of college life. Now with these students living in these trailers wearing dirty cut off jeans I'm sure that a lot of them have no concept of being what the old English would say was a gentleman in the late 80s. And Dean French thought to it that you got that much culture. Whether it's stuck or not I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think there was any difference in terms of the needs of students for getting this kind of culture? Whether they came from a rural or urban background?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: More came from rural backgrounds and probably is true today because we have fewer farms.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: We have larger farms and fewer farms. More city people. In other words Boise is a city of 100 ,000. Boise was a city of 17 ,000 when I came here. And we didn't have many big cities. There wasn't the work. It was more rural. There were smaller farms. You didn't have farm machinery. You didn't have combines. You threshed the wheat and put it in stacks. And then a stationary thresher was located in the field and you hauled the grain to the thresher. Well you could get by on a 60 acre farm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You haven't got six underdakers now. You might as well go out of business.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: These sons and daughters and the pioneers coming from rural areas were they more likely to be in the view of Dean French saying needing social races?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yes. I'm sure she felt that way.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: And I'm sure they needed it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'm sure they needed it.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: More so than they do now. We didn't have as many rich people in Idaho.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We didn't.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: All those people who had money would do as they still do. Send their sons.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you say? What? What kind of classes?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: They would send their students to Harvard or Yale or Stanford was quite a popular school. My wife for example went the first two years of her college work to University of California at Berkeley until her widowed mother she knew could no longer afford it. I don't think the mother ever said no but my wife was like I was smart enough to know that the tuition had gone up at California so she came to Idaho. Her junior year. But that was true that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Those who were well well.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Oh yes. People would tend to send their children. Out of state.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yes.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: To a better school. In other words they own just the way you used to go to Salt Lake to buy your clothes if you lived in the Pocatella area. The way that people do in Moscow now. Certain percentage of them go to Spokane to do their shopping stores here aren't quite good enough for them. They don't have the selection. That's been true.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So true. Talking about dating and activity between the sexes. In those days I'm curious about it in terms of the contrast with the way it is the way it is now. Was it usually that you would date with a lot of different people during your during your years in college or was it would you tend to be going steady.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: I think it hasn't changed. I think it's about the same. Except you did other things. Popular past time then was to walk the railroad tracks. Heavens yes you didn't have a car. There was no picture show on Sunday. The American Legion.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Was backer with the churches. No the churches kept Sunday movies out of Moscow until 1928 or 29 and then the American Legion. Persuaded the city council to allow the picture shows on Sunday and Mr Kenworthy gave for many years a percentage of his receipts from Sunday movies to the American Legion. But up until that time when I was in school there was no Sunday movie and you had a date with your girl on Sunday. What do you do. You don't sit around the sorority living room with 15 other couples. You can't be personal there. So you'd go out and you'd walk around town or you'd walk the railroad tracks. I walked the railroad tracks out toward Pullman time and time again. And or out towards the cemetery. I used to be a popular past time down the main walk the railroad tracks out toward the cemetery. Saunter along and you could talk to your girl. That was a popular past time. It was enjoyable. I got engaged when I was walking the railroad tracks on the Pullman road.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It's interesting because I know for instance at Elk River the decision of the church the community church prevailed on the city council to stop Sunday movies. Caused really bitter feelings on the part of the young people because of the isolation of the place. That they had been having the Sunday movies. Then there was nothing for them to do.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Well that's true with the students here. We had about I came here just after World War I and the enrollment jumped about 200 that year. Around 650 to 850. Big jump in enrollment. And that was quite a lot of students to toss on the community without anything to do. So all you could do is to walk. Railroad track was level and you didn't have to think about the few cars that would be going along a dusty road and so forth. And so you walked the railroad tracks and you'd see dozens of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of attitude did that foster in the students a slightly negative attitude towards law school?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No. There was nothing to do. No. The students nowadays complained there's nothing to do. I know it. I know it.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: But my gosh the student union with the bowling alleys and the pool tables and the coffee bars and records to play to dance and such and such a place. We had none of those. Nothing left. The only thing you'd go is to the blue bucket and get a ice cream soda and sit there until somebody else wanted your table and get up and get out. Or you'd walk. We used to know like and I used to walk around this part of town and you just walk and saunter and as long as you got back by a certain hour why that was that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In those days do you think that there was much sex before marriage. Nowadays it's relatively common. It wasn't as common then as it is now. No. No.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Good girls didn't do it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Frankly.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: There was some certainly there has been since Adam but it wasn't as accepted as it is now. No student. No two students lived together as they do now. We know now there are hundreds of them. I see him at the grocery store. There are hundreds of them living together. That was not done. There was sex on campus I suppose. I know one of the houses. I won't name it. But the rumor spread real rapidly that the baby was found in a dresser drawer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's true.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: The girl gave birth to a baby in the sorority house and stuck it in the dresser drawer. I forget I believe the baby was I don't know what the baby lived or died. I forget. But it was spread like wildfire over the campus. It was true. And there were certain gals who were giving out as you say as the same as there is now. But it was not as common as it is by one tenth. A few thought you could live with a girl why she was being French would see that she was out tomorrow morning.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They kicked him out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I imagine it must have been difficult to keep things from being French. It was. It was. Her rapport with the wind. Oh she she knew what was going on.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Heaven's honor. She knew every couple that was going together period how she knew I don't know. She knew she knew my girl was Ruth Hawkins. There's no question about it. She knew she knew what every man who was his girl was and she knew every girl who had more or less a steady date.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She knew. Well in those days this is something that strikes me from from reading that I've done then as compared to now. Do you think that men were were expecting nothing like today men are never expected to get involved in brothels or anything like that. That's a thing of the past as far as the experience is going on. But do you think on the campus in those days that was more accepted for what men would do for to get experience. It was no more accepted but I think more men did it. Yes.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: They used to hike off to lowest. Lewis and was a lumberman's and mining town. The miners and lumbermen would come into lowest and once every six weeks to blow their wages blowing in. Yes. And there were a certain number of houses and certain students would see to that themselves. They would take care of it. They go down to lowest on the train or otherwise. Yes. Not a great percentage of them but a few. But if they propositioned their own girl while they get slapped in the face and that was that. My loser. That's right. They lose her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know there's a lot of discussion of women's liberation these days. And I'm wondering about about how much equality between the sexes there was in those days whether I know for instance that women didn't seem to have the opportunities to get involved in a lot of different fields of sex. I'm wondering about the study that they do today. Their career opportunities seem to have been relatively restricted. That's right.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Well it was unthinkable almost. So he began to get a little nervous and he called up say Twin Falls or Rupert. Whether he heard that the plane had been seen going over or not I do not remember but along about six o 'clock or six thirty why then it was apparent that the plane was down. And the telephone office where the Intermountain Telephone Company was right next door to the statesman building. So Miller went over about six thirty or seven o 'clock and saw the chief operator and asked her to put one of the long distance operators at our disposal. He gave her instructions to contact through other exchanges every farmer in Southwest Idaho who had a telephone. And so he'd get on one phone and I was on the other and some of them had seen the plane and most of them had not. And so in those days radio is used earphones on radio was just the beginning of radio in 1925 and radio was an oddity and very few people had them. And so we decided he decided I didn't he decided to put out a special edition and get it out about ten o 'clock and go up to the big dance at the Capitol and sell them and sell them on the streets. So we put out a special edition and that means he just changed the front page enough to get one story on it and the rest of it was just the same as yesterday's paper. And we put out a special and I learned a very valuable lesson that you never anticipate the news. Well they didn't hear from the man for five days and he landed over in Jordan Valley. And he wrecked the plane and it took him five days to walk out to a farmhouse where they had a telephone. I guess he went to a couple before that but he finally walked out to where they had a telephone. His name was Rose. Don't know his first name. And this was the return flight. Return flight. Yes. The first flight was south to Salt Lake and the return flight was Salt Lake to Pasco. And it was return flight.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And the other newspaper was there another newspaper?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yes.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: They had already gone out with the story that. There was an afternoon paper and they included in their story the fact that the morning plane came in and there was a big celebration and so forth. That the afternoon plane came in approximately on time and deposited its package and had gone on to Pasco. Of course there was no celebration for the afternoon flight. But they just included in their story that the flight had gone on to Pasco. And it must have caused consternation at the dance. Oh yes it did. But it made the other paper look silly. Because they anticipated the news you see. But he had run into a thunder and lightning storm over the O 'waihea Mountains and he had tried to skirt it. And got lost and of course at that time all they had was an ordinary compass. And he got lost and didn't know where he was and put down on the field. Down in Jordan Valley. If ever went through Jordan Valley you know you could walk for five days and not find anything. So he had walked out and I suppose he had, I don't know how he walked in circles or what, but he finally walked out and got to a telephone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get a lot of good newspaper experience working on the space?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Oh yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yes.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: It was a good paper then. I don't see it now anymore. But it was a, of course, newspaper in those days was much different than it is now. When I ran the Idahoan, if you've looked at the issues for the ten years before I quit, I had a rule of twenty -five stories on page one. Now they run four stories on page one. The Idaho statesman when I worked there would run forty to fifty stories on page one. And I still think that is preferable. I have yet to find anyone among my friends who read these long in -depth stories. There was one last night, I can't read them all of them. I read that much and I'm all through. They run them for two columns. I don't think one person in fifty reads the whole thing. Except those about whom it's written or their friends. But I prefer to see them ten inch stories and run a lot of them. Give them the news. I get more dead news out of the Wall Street Journal than I do out of any newspaper. Because they got one column right there. And they carry more news of the world and that much than you get out of any daily paper that I see. And I would always, I tried to get twenty -five on page one. I didn't always make it. Sometimes it would be eighteen, sometimes twenty -one. But I would run a half a column story from the top of the page to the fold. That was about enough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was your job at Boise on the paper?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: City editor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that a demanding job? Did you try to cover the city's own city?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, no.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: At that time the Idaho Statesman had seven reporters. One Glenn Bauch who is still living. And he writes children's stories and he has for the last forty years. He was writing circuit. He'd spend one day in Glens Ferry and one day in Rupert and one day in Twin Falls and one day in Gooding and then about ten o 'clock at night he would phone in his news. And once every month he'd drop into Boise for a Sunday. He was single. And we had one man road circuit from Boise to Caldwell and Nampa and back on the electric interurban line. He did that every day. He'd leave about nine o 'clock in the morning and ride the stop off at Caldwell and get the local news at Caldwell at the courthouse and so forth and go to Nampa. The trains ran about once an hour. Just one car called up the interurban.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that where they would get the news at the courthouse?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: At the courthouse or he'd go to the Chamber of Commerce or any place else. Once in a while he'd stop at Meridian and once in a while he'd stop at Starr and get local news. Now of course they do it by having a paid correspondent and so forth. But the city editor I was made assignments to the other three reporters probably or four if there was something special. And the copy came to me and I would edit it and write the headlines on it and pass it over to the managing editor. And if he liked it he would take it as is. If he didn't like it he would cut it himself or say I don't like the headline lawyer or so forth and so on. That was that. He was managing editor. He ran it. And the opposite to me was a telegraph editor who had the telegraph news. He would write that, the world news. And the managing editor would find out from the business manager or the composing room the number of inches we had available for news. So at seven o 'clock at night he would say we've got a hole of so many inches. And the city would have 40 % of that hole and the telegraph editor would have 60 % or vice versa. And you had a sheet and every story that came in I was allowed say 1500 inches and I would take every story and estimate that it would be five inches or seven inches or so forth and keep her running total of the numbers of words or numbers of inches and just the beginning of the headline so I'd know what it is. I could look back and see what the story was and when you got full you quit. That was the job. I don't know how they do it now I suppose about the same way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well before we get into the newspaper business we should save that for next time.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: I don't know if I'm helping you or not. Women wouldn't be in a college of engineering. There were no women in the college of engineering and if a girl had enrolled in the college of engineering the dean would have done everything to discourage her. It was a man's feeling. I remember Bernice Day whose father who had this big house on the corner here the day home and he and his brothers owned the day mine in the court of lanes and had a big stock in the bunker hill and Sullivan mine. Jerome Day was one of the discoverers of the bunker hill on Sullivan mine and he had a daughter he had a son and a daughter and his son was drowned on Lake Washington in Seattle as the boy of about 19 and it broke his father's heart and he had a daughter and she was huge in size and she took mining engineering. And that was just scandal of the campus but she did it and she graduated but it was almost unheard of she was a freak to take mining engineering. She died a number of years ago. She's younger than I am but she died because she was huge in size and that was just a dead immortal since she wouldn't live to old age.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's the only thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why do you think the attitudes were as they were at that time about that sort of thing?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It certainly doesn't seem sinful to me for a woman who wants to study mining engineering to take it but that's my generation. Well the woman's place was in the home.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: She should take home economics or English or languages or some of the fine arts courses. She wasn't supposed to earn the living and men earned the living and she was supposed to stay home and raise the family.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So why take engineering?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: She couldn't get a job. But now it's different you see you've got equality but in those days you couldn't get a job if you were a woman engineer. Hopeless. You couldn't practice law because you'd be ostracized. There were no women lawyers in those days. So there were certain just the same as a boy would never enroll in home economics. A few of them do now. Just the way the world was built.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: It's different.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you feel that in an intellectual plane that women were regarded as less competent?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: In certain fields yes. It bothered the hell out of me when Dean Angel's daughter was enrolled in my calculus course as a sophomore. I was a sophomore in engineering. She majored in mathematics and she was in my calculus course. The only woman I ever had in any course related to engineering and she's a good friend of mine. There's two doors down here. Mary Evelyn Angel do so. But she took calculus in my course and the men resented it. I resented it. Probably because she was smarter than most of the men. Probably true. But they just didn't like it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They didn't like it.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: I can tell you a story if you're interested about the teacher of that course. Professor Eugene Taylor. I think it was in the calculus course that he announced at the beginning of the year that he had been teaching for 17 years. He had never missed a class in his life. He had never been late for a class in his life. And he said that if any time the bell rings and I'm not in here in my desk it means that something unusual has happened and that's that. You may be free to go. On the other hand if one of you students is not in his chair when the bell rings you're not present. You can stay but you're not present. And that was that. And throughout the course he'd be lecturing to put in something on the blackboard and the rail would ring and he'd lay his chalk down. That's the end of the course. He never kept you at five seconds overtime. One day the bell rang and Professor Taylor was not in the room. So we all get up and walked out. Open the door and there was Professor Taylor just coming up. Morning Mr. Taylor. Morning gentlemen. We kept on going. The next morning he said the gentleman. I made a mistake. He said I've been in the habit of accepting a ride from one of my neighbors. Dean messenger of the College of Engineering. Yesterday we had a flat tire. I was late. Gentlemen from now on I shall walk. And he walked to class. He lived over here just as far from the campus as I do. He walked to class every day until he retired about 40 years later. He never rode. I used to go to work in the morning at 730 and I'd pass him going down 3rd Street. He walked the rest of his life.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: A few years after he only died about two years ago up in his 90s but about five or six years ago I was at a cocktail party of a friend who lived right near there and the tailors were invited. And I saw Mrs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Taylor there and I said you know I got a good story to tell about your husband and I started to tell her and she says my God were you in that class?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: And I said yes. She said we have talked about that many and many a time. Go over and tell Gene that you were in that class which I did. And he enjoyed it. But everyone knows that the Eugene Taylor walked to and from class every day for 40 years and that's the reason.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This passion for punctuality was that the way he taught as well?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It's the way he taught. Yes sir.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: He flunked me in an exam one time. I think it was calculus. It might have been engineering mathematics I don't know. And he asked me to stay after class and he gave me a flunking. Oh a periodic exam. It wasn't the final exam. And he said Mr. Bose he said I flunked you on that course and he said you did the problem right. But you made a mistake in arithmetic. He said you know you're a poor enough student you can't afford that. From that day to this I bragged I never make a mistake in arithmetic. I do occasionally but in particular I see that the arithmetic is correct because I did the problem right but I got the wrong answer. He didn't give me anything he gave me zero. Some professors would have given you half credit for doing the problem right but having made a mistake in arithmetic. He lived mathematics. His life was mathematics and the time he got up until the time he went to bed he was prompt. He lived mathematics and he is my favorite professor of all. He and Dr. Costelli in chemistry are my two favorite professors. They were different entirely different. What is it about them that well we were just good teachers. They were just excellent teachers. Everyone thought that Taylor was an excellent teacher. He was an excellent teacher.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Does that help you. By excellent you mean to get it across. Oh yes. Definitely.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: He'd work with you to answer questions you could go to his office anytime and he would take time out to explain things. And he was never too busy to help you but he lived by the rules. If you weren't in your seat when the bell rang you were not present.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's that.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: That means you couldn't ask anything. You couldn't ask any questions. You couldn't turn the paper in. You could not participate in the discussion. You could listen but you couldn't do anything else. We're not present. And believe me everybody was in your seat when the course started.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you first came to Moscow what did you think of the town as a freshman student here?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: It was a lousy town. We all made fun of it. It was small. Didn't have any good stores. The movie theater was a small theater on 3rd Street. And before I graduated they had the one down on Main just one of them. But it was a poor town. No one liked it. They all made fun of little Moscow. But of course actually it was a nice little town. It's the way you believe you don't. Home is best. Home is good. Home is fine. This is no place to be.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well it certainly wasn't very urban even compared to Boise at the time I imagine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you come involved in the fraternity rather quickly after you came here?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The second year.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Sigma Chi.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What made you decide on that?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: There were only about four fraternities at that time. There was room for more. And during that period there were three or four fraternities started. Sigma Chi was started. Alpha Chi Omega was started. The Pi Phi's were started. And TKEs later there were four or five started. At that time there were only about four. Kappa C, Beta,

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Phi Delta, Theta, and Sigma Nu. Those were the only fraternities we had.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were the advantages to a student then of being in a fraternity?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just to say this is now. No different.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Friendship and place to live and so forth and so on. Just exactly the same as now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I understand that the freshmen were in a somewhat disadvantaged position with regard to the older students. Oh surely.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: They were razzed. They've given up that now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Mostly.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had to wear green hats?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: All freshmen on campus had to wear green hats. If you didn't wear a green hat on campus you'd got paddled by the upperclassmen.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: I mean paddle if they're upperclassmen. Junior or senior. Caught to freshmen without. And we were all known. Everybody knew who the freshmen were. If they caught you without a green beanie. Someone would rush and get a paddle and bend you over their knees and paddle you.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: They had traditions. Of course that's what made the institution. Juniors and seniors could wear corduroy trousers. Every junior and senior wore a corduroy pair of pants. And as soon as you got to be a junior the first thing you did was to pay six dollars for a pair of corduroy pants. The rule was you never washed them. The dirtier they got the better. Which is what they do now with their jeans. That was fashion. That was fashion. You didn't wash them if they stood up by themselves. That's fine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'm surprised Dean French tolerated that but then I guess she was Dean of Women. She's Dean of Women.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: And of course the seniors carried kings. Senior men carried kings.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Yes. I didn't think they would be that old. Well just as a distinction. You knew he was a senior because he carried the king.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the senior snake? Was that a real institution?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: No. They passed the word around or else have a meeting. I think it was a senior class meeting and they decide on a certain day. And of course the faculty was always advised of it. They weren't supposed to be but they always knew and so that day the seniors would sneak out of the house early and have rented two or three vans or a stage or someplace and go to Coeur d 'Alene or Lewiston or Elk River or something for a picnic. And get home about nine o 'clock at night. It was a senior sneak. And you were not docked for having missed class. It was just one of the things that was done, a senior sneak. I guess the sororities do it to this day. Maybe the sororities and fraternities do it to now. I don't know. They did for a good many years later but not the whole class.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the home fight? Or how do you say that?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Hume. H -U -M -E. Well, it was named for Dean Hume who was a very, very popular teacher. I never took a course under him because I was in engineering. I didn't have time. He was a very popular teacher. And he had a world reputation. And he was a good Joe and he encouraged them to have a Hume fight. It was a case of getting along rope and straining across Paradise Creek and the freshmen on one side and the soft boars on the other and see who would get dumped. And then during the evening, the freshmen would roam the town looking for sophomores to paddle them and the sophomores would look for the freshmen to paddle them. And they formed an army and they'd march around Main Street, nothing down third. And the sophomores were always in the minority so they usually kept out of the way, although both sides claimed victory. That was that. Something to do, there wasn't anything else to do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It sounds like institutionalizing what otherwise could be more rowdy and disorganized.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the rivalry with Washington State?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: It was very severe. More so than now, when Washington State would play Idaho in football, they would wear their Rooter's Cats, the WSU Cat, gray and red with a WSU across it. The object was for Idaho students to swipe them. And when Idaho went to Pullman, we went by train. They hired a special train, three or four cars, and they'd all go over to WSU by train and march from the train to the campus for the football game, march as a body. And we all wore our eye caps. And the object was for WSU students to swipe an eye cap. And so some men, possibly some women, would stray five feet away from the line of march while some WSU student would dart out and grab the cap. He'd be protected by his fellow students until he got away. I had a WSU cap for years. I don't know what happened to it, but I got one someplace.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't Idaho really badly outmatched in football?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Not as badly then as we are now. Not as badly then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the bonfire? Was that a regular?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: A regular event the night before the WSU game. They had the bonfire on what is now Gormley Field. That time I called it Recreation Park, but it was just a open field. There was no baseball diamond. There was nothing on it. It was just a piece of ground. And for a week they would gather, use lumber, use boxes, cardboard boxes, wood, anything they could possibly get to build a huge bonfire about the size of this house. And they always had to be topped with a privy. So then about eight o 'clock at night, then it got dark while they'd light the bonfire and have a rally. The cheerleaders would lead the students in rallies singing the Idaho Fight Song and cheering the team. And after 40 minutes it would break up and the fire would die down and somebody would watch it through the night so it wouldn't spread. That was that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just a bonfire.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: He had a lot of those things. He had to do something to amuse the students. Besides now they go to the beer halls.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: One other one that struck me reading Beacon of Mountain Plain was the Pajama Parade.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Well, the Pajama Parade I think was...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was through downtown people. It looked to me as though it was kind of a human chain. Yes.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: It seems to me it was only girls, but I could be wrong. Maybe it was boys and girls. It wasn't downtown so much, but it was through the dormitories and halls. And they would be led by the cheerleaders and they all wore pajamas and stocking caps and they would parade through the house at dinner time or right after dinner and yelling and shouting and swiping souvenirs on the way and so forth and so on. It was just a matter of letting off steam, but cheering the team. In other words, it was an expression of student unity and student support for the football team or the basketball team. I don't recall whether the Pajama Parade was before the basketball team or the football. I mean the basketball game or the football game.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think it was football.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How strong would you say looking back at it was the community spirit at the University?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Much stronger.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you call it a real community?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yes.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: The store is always closed for the Idaho WSU game. The store would never stay open for the community. I don't know if they stay open now or not. Some of them may close, but it was just universal. They just closed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But on the campus itself, among the student body, with the small size of the student body being as it was, did that make a very closely knit?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yes, I think it did, decidedly.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: For basketball games, I was writing for half of us in newspapers my junior and senior year. I had a string of papers and we had to file them by telegraph. The Associated Press was a lot slower than it is now because it was operated by the Morse code. That's true. The Spokesman Review had, the night main wire was taken down in Morse code by an operator. I worked on the Idaho Statesman when I quit the Westinghouse Electric Company for a year. They received their Associated Press news by Morse code. I don't know how fast a man could operate, maybe 25 words a minute. When they got teletypes, it was 60 words a minute. Now, I don't know how fast it is, but it's a lot faster to get more news. The papers that served Idaho and attempted to cover the news, they would have correspondence. I wrote for the Salt Lake Tribune, the Portland Oregonian, the Seattle Post -Intelligent Serve, and the Seattle Times and the Spokesman Review. I would try to write two stories so that they wouldn't see each other. My wife and I, at the end of the basketball game, we'd run to the telegraph office to get ahead of the one or two other correspondence that we wrote for other newspapers. Joel Priest, a good friend of mine, had the Idaho Statesman, and I guess he had the other paper in Salt Lake. The first that got there, well, it was the first one to have the privilege of getting it on the wire. We used to run down to the... Of course, the basketball games were then played, and what is now the Women's Gym. It only held five or six hundred people. We used to stand in line for an hour to get in. When we played the Pacific Coast Conference games here, and I wrote that up for the brochure at the dedication of Kibbe -Dome. I don't know if you saw that little brochure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Haven't you seen it?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: No, I knew about it. Well, I wrote a story in there on the support of athletics in the early days. We won the Pacific Coast Conference, and we were in the Coast Conference. We defeated California in best two out of three games, and it was held here. Of course, the Californians from Berkeley thought that the gym was a cracker box, but it was a standard size basketball court. But the seats were right up to the line. I think we went out to take a sandwich with us, but we went to stand in line about five o 'clock in the afternoon to get in.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: The game didn't start until, say, eight o 'clock or seven thirty, and we'd stand there for two hours to get in. And of course, we won the conference championship. There was a Northern Division and the Southern Division, and we defeated Washington for the Northern Division, and California defeated, I don't know who, for the Southern Division. And then the championship was decided here, next year to be go to the Southern Division, but we had it here, and we won the title two years, Coast Championship for the Pacific Coast Conference. One year on strictly percentage basis, and the other year by meeting the divisional leaders.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was up on the president at that time when you were?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: I believe so. Lindley was the president when I first came here. I can't think of his name now. He went from here to Kansas. Ernest K. Lindsey. He had a son here who was Ernest K. Lindsey, Jr. And he played basketball. He was a big fellow, about six foot three. And he later became editor of Newsweek magazine. And he was the biographer of Franklin D. Roosevelt. And he's been out here, he had been out here a couple times. To renew acquaintances in April, they had the fiftieth anniversary of it. It was publicized.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you notice, did you see it?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Oh yes, of the Air Mail flight. Well that was the first flight. Yeah. That was the first Air Mail flight in the nation. And it was Varney Airlines. And they were to start from Pasco going east to Salt Lake, and then transfer from Salt Lake to faster train going east. And then start from Salt Lake West going to Pasco, and from Pasco to be distributed to Seattle and Portland by train. And Boise made quite a celebration of it. I was on the city desk of the Idaho State School, and they had a big parade in the morning. I'm guessing at ten o 'clock. And of course in those days, on every occasion, you had a parade. That was the thing to do. And they scheduled an outdoor dance on the plaza in front of the state capitol for that evening. Well, the Air Mail plane came in from Pasco at 11, 11, 15, maybe 11, 30 in the morning. And the pilot was Leon Cuddyback, who later became the administrator for the FAA at Seattle. He's still living. He's past 80, but he's still living. And he was in Boise a few weeks ago for the celebration. And the mayor was there at the airfield, which is where Boise College now is. And it was a small biplane. And the mayor was there, and I believe Senator Boro was there, and there was a small package of mail for Boise, and then a larger package for Salt Lake. He had an afternoon paper there called the Capitol News. Well, my photographer and I were on top of the Hawaii Hotel, and he took some pictures of the parade, and then we went down to the field, and he got a picture of the first Air Mail pouch being handed to the mayor and so forth. And the Capitol News went to press around 2 .30 is an afternoon paper. Of course, they had the break on the story, and so they wrote the story up. They had pictures, of course, of the event, and they told how the first mail came in on time, and the package was given to the mayor. And the northbound flight came in at 2 o 'clock from Salt Lake and went on to Pasco and so forth, and that was that. And along about 4 o 'clock, I began to hear rumors that the plane coming north had not arrived. And the managing editor was Horatio Miller, and he wrote under the byline of Cato the Sensor. And he was managing editor, and so he began to get interested, and about a quarter to five, he became more interested, and we can see off in the southwest over the Hawaii Mountains, which were about 90 miles south of Boise, the dark black clouds. So he put a call in to Salt Lake and found out that the plane had left.
